observation.summary: DKS as an FSM has three terminal acceptance states (closed loop, short-circuit, gated), and the corpus-FSM carries history via a warrant thread across cycles, making it not strictly Markovian — whether to formalise this as a finite-state automaton with scratchpad or to model it as a stateful process with formal memory structure is open.

warrant.claim: The DKS corpus-FSM should be formalised as a finite-state automaton with scratchpad (FSAS) rather than as an open-ended stateful process with formal memory structure, because the FSAS model preserves the level-separation condition required for legitimate meta-level adjudication while bounding the complexity of the warrant thread to a tractable, auditable artefact.
warrant.data: The DKS FSM already exhibits three terminal acceptance states (closed loop, short-circuit, gated) and carries warrant-thread history across cycles. This cross-cycle memory is the sole feature that breaks strict Markovian behaviour. A scratchpad augmentation to a classical FSM is the minimal, well-understood extension that accommodates bounded auxiliary memory without abandoning the finite-control structure; the resulting FSAS remains fully specifiable, its state-transition function remains separable from its memory-access operations, and its acceptance conditions remain decidable within the original three-terminal schema.
warrant.warrant: Any warrant called upon to adjudicate its own scope must be suspended from operative use during that adjudication and replaced by a frame imported from a level not currently under revision; only warrants whose scope does not include the present revision operation may legitimately evaluate that operation. This Level-Separation Condition prevents reflexivity collapse by ensuring no inferential mechanism serves simultaneously as both the object of a meta-claim and the evaluator of that meta-claim's validity. An FSAS architecture enforces this condition structurally: the finite control (meta-level) and the scratchpad (object-level history) occupy distinct, non-overlapping derivation layers, so the transition function that governs warrant evaluation is never itself written into the scratchpad it reads.
warrant.backing: Classical automata theory establishes that a finite-state automaton augmented with a bounded auxiliary store remains in a strictly lower complexity class than a general Turing-equivalent process, and that its acceptance conditions are decidable. The Level-Separation Condition drawn from the available warrants requires that the adjudicative frame not be subject to the rules under revision; in an FSAS this is guaranteed by architectural fiat—the control states and scratchpad are defined at syntactically distinct levels. By contrast, an open-ended stateful process with formal memory structure conflates the two levels: the memory structure can in principle encode and revise the very transition rules that govern its own evaluation, violating the Level-Separation Condition and generating the structural pathology identified in the warrant corpus (a warrant lacking reflexive inertness or a grounded termination rule is structurally pathological and must be suspended). The conservative FSAS choice therefore satisfies the meta-level requirements already endorsed by the existing warrant thread without introducing novel, unanchored machinery.
warrant.qualifier: Presumably—this formalisation is adequate provided the empirical warrant thread does not in practice require unbounded memory growth; should future cycles demonstrate that the scratchpad bound is systematically insufficient to preserve warrant-thread coherence across the observed corpus, the FSAS model must be re-examined, and the residual circularity between the memory-bound posit and the adequacy criterion it underwrites must be explicitly flagged as a pragmatic regress-stopper subject to contestation.

argument_a.evidence: The three available warrant claims bearing directly on level-separation (Level-Separation Condition warrant, the meta-level boundary-establishment warrant, and the reflexive-inertness warrant) all converge on the requirement that the adjudicative frame be structurally distinct from the object under evaluation. An FSAS instantiates this separation by construction. No available warrant supports the stronger move to an unrestricted formal-memory model, and the warrant requiring epistemic systems to flag residual circularity and bound confidence proportionally to derivation-tree overlap actively counsels against adopting a model whose memory structure could encode its own governing rules.

warrant.claim: Modelling DKS as a finite-state automaton with scratchpad is preferable to a purely Markovian FSM, but this preference is itself only warrantable from a meta-level frame that is declared as a pragmatic posit rather than a derived necessity, making the formalisation choice revisable rather than settled.
warrant.data: The observation establishes that DKS has three terminal acceptance states and that the corpus-FSM carries warrant-thread history across cycles, violating the Markov property. Two candidate formalisations are in competition: (A) FSM-with-scratchpad, which grafts a bounded external memory onto a finite-state core, and (B) a stateful process with formal memory structure (e.g., pushdown automaton, register machine, or labelled transition system with explicit history). Neither formalisation is neutral with respect to the adequacy criteria used to evaluate it: FSM-with-scratchpad preserves the tractability of finite-state analysis while externalising history, whereas a stateful-process model makes history intrinsic but complicates decidability and model-checking. The choice between them is not resolvable by appeal to descriptive fit alone (both can be made to fit the observed behaviour) nor by normative coherence from within the DKS framework (DKS is itself the object under formalisation).
warrant.warrant: A well-formed meta-principle must absorb rather than pre-empt productive indeterminacy in granularity: it achieves authority not by claiming to logically derive the boundary conditions it adjudicates, but by explicitly marking those boundary posits as contingent, revisable, and pragmatically motivated—thereby avoiding the structural regularity whereby warrants requiring prior logical settlement of their own enabling conditions are systematically self-undermining. Applied here: the adequacy criterion that selects between the two formalisations (tractability of the reflexive warrant-thread relationship versus expressive completeness of the memory model) must itself be declared a pragmatic posit. Doing so makes the preference for FSM-with-scratchpad defensible—because it makes the co-constitutive loop tractable—without claiming that tractability is a logically necessary criterion rather than a chosen one.
warrant.backing: The warrant cluster from the available set converges on two points: (1) when the domain of application is isomorphic to the evaluative processes used to assess it, adequacy criteria cannot be validated from within (second-order tractability replaces first-order fit); and (2) any meta-level frame must explicitly mark its own regress-stopping status as a pragmatic posit, naming the level at which grounding is suspended and remaining open to revision. FSM-with-scratchpad satisfies the tractability criterion because it separates state-transition logic (finite, inspectable) from memory (bounded scratchpad), giving analysts a structurally distinct handle on the warrant thread without collapsing the level boundary. The stateful-process model preserves more expressive power but at the cost of this level-separation, potentially re-entangling the evaluative frame with the object of evaluation. The preference for FSM-with-scratchpad is therefore grounded in the pragmatic posit that level-separation tractability outweighs expressive completeness at this stage of DKS formalisation—a posit that must remain explicitly flagged and contestable.
warrant.qualifier: Probably, and subject to revision if: (a) the scratchpad is shown to require unbounded growth in practice, collapsing the finite-state guarantee; (b) a stateful-process model is developed that independently establishes its own level-separation condition; or (c) the pragmatic posit favouring tractability is successfully contested by showing that expressive incompleteness of the scratchpad model generates systematic distortions in warrant-thread representation that undermine DKS's core function.

argument_b.evidence: Turing's original scratchpad abstraction (tape-as-external-memory) demonstrates that separating control state from memory is a productive formal move that preserves analytical traction without sacrificing expressiveness up to Turing-completeness—though bounded scratchpads sacrifice the latter, which is precisely what makes them tractable for model-checking. Pushdown automata and register machines, by contrast, embed memory in the transition structure, making reachability and acceptance undecidable in the general case. For DKS, where the warrant thread is finite in any given corpus cycle even if unbounded across all cycles, the bounded scratchpad is empirically adequate and analytically tractable, supporting the pragmatic preference without claiming it as logically necessary.

contradicts.reason: Claim mismatch: A asserts 'The DKS corpus-FSM should be formalised as a finite-state automaton with scratchpad (FSAS) rather than as an open-ended stateful process with formal memory structure, because the FSAS model preserves the level-separation condition required for legitimate meta-level adjudication while bounding the complexity of the warrant thread to a tractable, auditable artefact.'; B asserts 'Modelling DKS as a finite-state automaton with scratchpad is preferable to a purely Markovian FSM, but this preference is itself only warrantable from a meta-level frame that is declared as a pragmatic posit rather than a derived necessity, making the formalisation choice revisable rather than settled.'

counter.counter_claim: The Level-Separation Condition cannot be grounded in the FSAS architecture itself without circularity, because the claim that finite control and scratchpad occupy 'distinct, non-overlapping derivation layers' is itself a structural assertion that must be evaluated by some meta-level criterion—yet the warrant offers no independent basis for choosing that criterion other than the pragmatic posit that FSAS is preferable to a purely Markovian FSM.
counter.reason: The warrant purports to derive the Level-Separation Condition as a structural necessity enforced by the FSAS architecture, but the attacker's claim exposes that the preference for FSAS over a Markovian FSM is itself only warrantable from a meta-level frame declared as a pragmatic posit. This means the architectural enforcement the warrant relies on is not a derived necessity but a revisable design choice. The warrant therefore fails to deliver the logical force it claims: if the structural separation is contingent on a pragmatic posit at a higher level, then 'structural enforcement' is not sufficient grounding for the Level-Separation Condition, and the warrant collapses into the same reflexivity problem it was meant to solve—it must appeal to an unchosen, externally imported frame to justify the very frame-separation it asserts.

pattern.description: The contradiction exposes a self-referential grounding loop: any architecture that partitions its own components into 'distinct layers' must invoke a meta-level criterion to validate that partition, but if the architecture is itself the sole warrant for the criterion, the justification is circular. The structural regularity is that layered-separation claims within a single formal system cannot be simultaneously (a) internal to the system and (b) foundationally independent of it—every intra-system boundary assertion silently presupposes an external vantage point that the system itself cannot supply. This pattern generalises: whenever a formalism uses its own topology to justify the legitimacy of that topology, it collapses the object-level and meta-level into one undifferentiated stratum, undermining the very separation it asserts.

revised_warrant.claim: Any formal system that asserts the legitimacy of its own internal layer-boundaries must explicitly invoke an external or metatheoretic criterion that is not itself derived solely from the system's own topology.
revised_warrant.data: The self-referential grounding loop demonstrates that intra-system boundary assertions simultaneously require (a) an internal definition of the boundary and (b) a foundationally independent warrant for that definition—conditions that cannot both be satisfied by appeal to the same system alone. Gödel's incompleteness results, Tarski's undefinability theorem, and Russell's type-theoretic regress all instantiate this structural regularity: no sufficiently expressive formal system can supply its own complete metatheoretic foundation.
revised_warrant.warrant: A layered-separation claim is epistemically valid if and only if the criterion used to validate the partition is grounded in a metatheoretic frame that is (i) explicitly distinguished from the object system, (ii) not derived solely from that system's internal axioms or topology, and (iii) acknowledged as provisional relative to any higher metatheoretic frame that may in turn be required. When these conditions are absent, the architecture collapses object-level and meta-level into a single undifferentiated stratum, and the separation claim is self-undermining. Future cycle designs must therefore treat every boundary-assertion as carrying an implicit metatheoretic commitment that must be surfaced, named, and located outside the system being partitioned.
revised_warrant.backing: Tarski (1936) on the hierarchy of metalanguages; Gödel (1931) incompleteness theorems establishing limits of intra-system self-justification; Russell & Whitehead (1910) theory of types as a response to self-referential paradox; Quine (1951) on the regress of analytic/synthetic distinctions; Hofstadter (1979) on strange loops and tangled hierarchies; category-theoretic accounts of adjunctions as external validators of internal structure (Mac Lane, 1971).
revised_warrant.qualifier: This revised rule holds for all formal systems expressive enough to represent their own syntax (per Gödel's diagonal lemma conditions). For systems deliberately constructed below that expressiveness threshold, internal boundary criteria may be sufficient—but only because the system has been externally constrained to prevent self-reference, which is itself a metatheoretic act performed outside the system.
